name: Python
scope: An individual at BASIC level, with 0-2 years of experience, understands Python syntax and basic programming concepts like variables, data types (integers, floats, strings, booleans), and operators (arithmetic, comparison, logical). They can use control flow statements (if, elif, else) and loops (for, while) to solve simple problems. They are capable of writing simple scripts to automate basic tasks, perform calculations, and handle file I/O operations like reading from and writing to text files. They can work with fundamental data structures such as lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets, and can define and call functions with arguments and return values. They know how to import and use standard library modules (e.g., math, random, datetime) and handle exceptions using try and except blocks. They use basic tools like text editors or simple IDEs (IDLE, Visual Studio Code), run scripts from the command line, and manage packages with pip. They can debug simple syntax and runtime errors using print statements, read and understand straightforward code written by others, and make minor edits to existing scripts under guidance. They are not expected to design complex systems but can follow predefined specifications and contribute to projects by handling straightforward tasks.

name: Redis
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Redis as a competency should be able to describe Redis as an in-memory, single-threaded, event-driven store, differentiate RDB snapshots vs AOF persistence and explain TTL, expiration and eviction interactions on key lifecycle. They can choose the correct structure—strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, HyperLogLog or GEO—for a problem, knowing memory and complexity costs. They are fluent with redis-cli, pipelining, MULTI/EXEC, Lua EVAL, INFO, MONITOR, CONFIG, SLOWLOG and redis-benchmark for atomicity, introspection and load tests. They integrate Redis via Java, Python, Node.js, Go, C# clients, tune pools, timeouts, pipelining, and serialize with UTF-8, JSON, MessagePack or Protobuf. They configure RDB schedules, AOF fsync and rewrite thresholds, run backups, restores, cold-start recovery and estimate load times and disk growth. They deploy primary-replica with Sentinel, set quorum, monitor replication lag/backlog, validate failover handling and are aware of cluster mode without designing shards. They enforce AUTH/ACLs, rotate passwords, enable TLS, run behind firewalls or container namespaces and avoid default-port exposure. They export metrics to Prometheus, DataDog or cloud monitors, build Grafana dashboards and alert on memory fragmentation, ops/sec, latency, hit ratio and client buffers with documented runbooks. They diagnose performance via INFO, SLOWLOG, tweak maxmemory, eviction policy, batching and pipelines, escalating deeper issues appropriately. They script SCAN-based key audits, bulk deletions, TTL checks and plan capacity around key count, memory and network throughput. They model caching, sessions, leaderboards, rate limiting, token buckets, distributed locks, pub/sub fan-out and metadata enrichment while discussing consistency, durability and stale-data trade-offs. They keep current through docs, release notes, community advisories and sandbox trials of features or modules such as RedisSearch and RedisJSON, knowing when to escalate to senior engineers.
long_scope:
Scope of Competency – BASIC Proficiency in Redis  
(Expected after roughly one–to–two years of focused, hands-on experience)

1. Conceptual Grounding  
A practitioner at this level possesses a solid mental model of what Redis is, where it sits in a typical application stack, and why it is chosen over alternative data stores in latency-critical paths. They know Redis is an in-memory, single-threaded, event-driven data platform that persists to disk for durability, and they can articulate the implications of that design on throughput, memory pressure and network I/O. They comfortably describe the lifecycle of a key, from creation through TTL expiration or eviction, and they recognize how Redis’ append-only and snapshot persistence strategies protect that lifecycle. The practitioner distinguishes at a glance between volatile, non-volatile and logical expiration of keys, and they understand how eviction policies interact with each of those forms of expiry.

2. Core Data-Structure Mastery  
The professional works confidently with the six primary native structures—strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets and bitmaps—understanding storage overhead, read/write complexity and the concurrency semantics that Redis guarantees for each. They can explain how atomicity is preserved through the event loop and can choose the right structure for a given modelling problem. Their knowledge extends to basic probabilistic structures such as HyperLogLog and simple geospatial indexing, though only to the degree of inserting, reading and deleting data while respecting performance trade-offs.

3. Fundamental Command Fluency  
Command-level fluency is second nature: they frame everyday operations in terms of single-key and multi-key commands, pipeline them for throughput, and know when a MULTI/EXEC transaction is necessary for atomicity versus when a Lua script is required to preclude race conditions. They wield INFO, MONITOR and CONFIG GET/SET for ad-hoc introspection and troubleshooting. They employ redis-cli interactively as well as in batch scripts and recognize when to fall back to redis-benchmark for empirical throughput measurement.

4. Application Integration  
The professional has integrated Redis into at least one production application using an idiomatic client in a general-purpose programming language (e.g., Java, C#, Python, Node.js, Go or Rust). They have dealt with connection-pool sizing, synchronous versus asynchronous APIs, simple pipelining and timeout handling. They factor the atomic semantics of Redis into their application’s concurrency model and guard against partial failures with retry logic and backoff. When presented with a serialization requirement they can select, configure and validate a codec (UTF-8 strings, JSON, MessagePack, Protocol Buffers, etc.) appropriate to the memory footprint and latency goals of the system.

5. Persistence, Durability and Recovery  
The practitioner can configure periodic RDB snapshots and append-only file persistence with reasonable fsync policies, weighing durability needs against latency budget. They perform manual and automated backup and restore exercises and have rehearsed recovery to a known point-in-time in a staging environment. They can size AOF rewrite thresholds to maintain growth within operational limits and react when AOF profusion threatens disk exhaustion. They appreciate that a cold start may render a node unavailable while loading the dataset and can compute approximate worst-case load times from memory footprint.

6. Basic High-Availability Topology Skills  
They comfortably deploy a primary-replica topology—stand-alone or orchestrated through Redis Sentinel—configure authentication, ACLs and simple TLS termination, and validate automatic failover in a sandbox. They monitor replication lag, disk backlog and master-link-status metrics, and recognize the operational impact of a promotion on client-side connection management. They understand Sentinel voting quorum requirements and configure minimally sufficient nodes for a single-data-centre deployment. They are aware of cluster mode but do not yet design, reshard or debug a fully sharded cluster.

7. Performance Awareness and First-Line Troubleshooting  
The professional keeps a watchful eye on memory fragmentation, resident versus virtual memory, instantaneous ops-per-second, average query latency and keyspace hit ratio. They formulate hypotheses around common performance pathologies (slow log growth, blocked client output buffers, saturation of the primary event loop) and confirm or reject those hypotheses through a disciplined use of INFO subsections and slow-log inspection. They can enact common first-aid countermeasures—memory policy tweaks, pipeline introduction, batched key deletion, appropriate maxmemory settings—while escalating deeper pathologies to more senior engineers.

8. Security Hygiene and Compliance Basics  
They operate Redis behind firewalled subnets or within container network namespaces, enforce AUTH or ACL- based authentication, and rotate credentials via configuration reloads. They configure TLS and client certificate validation where mandated. They separate operational roles through ACL categories (e.g., readonly, writeonly, admin) and audit those permissions. They are aware of—and do not leave—default-port, unauthenticated instances exposed.

9. Observability and Operations Toolkit  
The practitioner sets up dashboarding with a metrics backend (Prometheus exporters, DataDog integrations, Grafana panels, Cloud-vendor-native monitors, etc.) that surfaces memory, CPU, network throughput, latency and command-specific counters. They wire alerting thresholds aligned with service-level objectives and are accountable for runbooks describing immediate triage steps. They script routine housekeeping (e.g., keyspace scans, TTL audits, mass deletion of obsolete namespaces) under safe rate limits, and they document operational limits such as maximum key count and memory footprint per node for capacity planning.

10. Mental Catalogue of Canonical Use-Cases  
Beyond raw command knowledge, they can reason about how Redis accelerates real-time leaderboards, caching of rendered views, rate-limiting, token bucket enforcement, distributed locks, pub/sub message fan-out, session management and metadata enrichment. They can articulate trade-offs of each pattern, especially around cache eviction, stale-data exposure, idempotency and persistence guarantees, and they design minimal but correct data models for these scenarios.

11. Professional Disposition and Learning Trajectory  
Finally, the BASIC practitioner is habitually curious, able to consult documentation, release notes and community advisories to stay current with minor version changes, deprecation notes and emerging features. They appreciate when to escalate concerns to an intermediate or advanced colleague, document known gaps and consciously extend their knowledge by replicating advanced topologies or experimenting with modules such as RedisSearch or RedisJSON in a non-production sandbox.

A candidate meeting this scope can be trusted to configure, integrate, secure, operate and scale a single-node or primary-replica Redis deployment at moderate data volumes and traffic levels, while escalating deep performance tuning, complex data-model design, multi-cluster orchestration or module customization to more senior engineers.
